segments.title: The alternate-history case for Tiger Cubs
segments.summary: The scenario asks why Britain did not pair HMS Tiger with cheaper, 9.2-inch-gun cruisers while building dreadnoughts.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames the Tiger Cub idea as a plausible procurement alternative rather than a purely fanciful ship design. He argues that available slipways, armament production, and distressed British yards could have supported such ships, with government economy rather than industrial impossibility as the likely reason they were not ordered.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the Tiger Cub alternate-history proposal and identifies spare industrial capacity.

segments.title: HMS Tiger as the starting point
segments.summary: HMS Tiger's service dates, 13.5-inch armament, speed, and battlecruiser character set the benchmark for the proposed companion cruisers.
segments.body: The video begins the naval analysis with Tiger's 1911 order, 1912 laying down, 1913 launch, and October 1914 commissioning. Tiger is treated as an impressive and useful ship, but also as an expensive full battlecruiser whose qualities define what a lower-cost companion should complement rather than duplicate.

evidence.note: The transcript gives Tiger's construction timeline and describes her as a useful ship.
The discussion identifies Tiger as a 13.5-inch-gun battlecruiser.

segments.title: The spectrum from battlecruiser to battleship
segments.summary: The proposed ship sits below a battlecruiser in a design spectrum shaped by the trade between speed, armour coverage, and internal subdivision.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes cruiser-like battlecruisers, more protected battlecruisers, fast battleships, and full battleships. Armour thickness alone is not the point: the extent of protected hull and the subdivision behind it determine resilience, while speed is the usual price paid for greater protection.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the capital-ship spectrum and the protection-versus-speed trade-off.

segments.title: A 28-knot cruiser with 9.2-inch guns
segments.summary: The working Tiger Cub concept uses six to eight 9.2-inch guns, 4-inch secondaries, torpedoes, and Tiger-like 28-knot speed.
segments.body: The proposed ships are envisioned as fast, large cruisers rather than miniature battlecruisers. Dr. Clarke considers either a six-gun arrangement or four twin turrets, but regards eight 9.2-inch guns as the more likely fit if the ship is to match Tiger's speed while remaining within cruiser-like displacement limits.

evidence.note: The transcript outlines the projected armament, speed, secondary battery, torpedoes, and construction batches.

segments.title: Restoring the station-cruiser role
segments.summary: Tiger Cubs could supply a station flagship and deterrent presence that increasingly expensive battlecruisers could not provide in sufficient numbers.
segments.body: As Britain concentrates heavy units in European waters, it still needs ships that can represent power overseas without detaching a battlecruiser. A fast cruiser with eight 9.2-inch guns could overmatch most local opponents, create an intermediate step in escalation, and make negotiation more attractive before a crisis drew in the full Royal Navy.

evidence.note: The transcript links battlecruiser cost, overseas representation, station cruisers, and graduated deterrence.

segments.title: Small-tube boilers as a practical trial
segments.summary: A cheaper cruiser class could trial small-tube boilers without risking a capital ship, while still filling useful operational roles.
segments.body: The proposal gives the Royal Navy an opportunity to test higher-power machinery in ships that are valuable but less costly than battleships. Their peacetime station role makes an imperfect speed result more tolerable, while successful trials could inform later capital-ship design and help justify a class that replaces several older armoured-cruiser functions.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Jellicoe, boiler trials, cruiser risk, and the station-cruiser mission.

segments.title: Would mixed boiler batches be too risky?
segments.summary: A chat question asks whether the first class could compare small-tube and conventional boilers ship for ship.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke judges that the Royal Navy would probably accept the new machinery across the class rather than treat it as an intolerable experimental risk. Because the ships are station cruisers, missing the full 28-knot target would be less damaging than on a battleship, and the design offers a lower-risk place to gain confidence with the technology.
segments.question: Could the first four ships split between small-tube and conventional boilers to compare the technologies?
segments.answerShort: Probably not: the Navy would likely judge the cruiser trial acceptable across the class, since the ships remain useful station vessels even if they miss their ideal speed.

evidence.note: The transcript asks about mixed boiler batches and explains why the risk is lower for station cruisers.

segments.title: Idle battleship yards made the class buildable
segments.summary: Britain possessed both the political precedent and unused industrial capacity to order Tiger Cubs without displacing its dreadnought programme.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses the 1909 'We want eight' episode to show that even an economy-minded government could be pushed into funding more warships than the Admiralty initially requested. The industrial obstacle was also unusually weak because several British yards capable of battleship construction lacked enough orders and faced bankruptcy. A four-ship cruiser batch could therefore support distressed private capacity rather than compete for already saturated foreign yards. The passage makes Treasury restraint, not missing slips or weapons, the decisive reason this plausible class remained unbuilt.

evidence.note: The transcript connects the 1909 dreadnought funding campaign with idle British battleship-yard capacity.

segments.title: Benjamin Tillman and Charleston shipyard politics
segments.summary: A question about an American response turns into a case study in how local political power could shape naval construction.
segments.body: The question asks whether Senator Benjamin Tillman might seize on the British design as a reason to build comparable ships at Charleston. Dr. Clarke says that is plausible because Tillman's overriding incentives were reelection and political power in South Carolina. Expanding Charleston's yard and directing naval work there would serve those local interests even if the ships were not a national strategic priority. The exchange shows how constituency politics can redirect shipbuilding independently of fleet doctrine.
segments.question: Would Benjamin Tillman use the Tiger Cubs to justify building comparable ships at Charleston?
segments.answerShort: Possibly: Charleston construction and yard expansion would align closely with Tillman's local political incentives.

evidence.note: The transcript answers a question about Tillman, Charleston, reelection, and local shipbuilding power.

segments.title: A global-presence ship, not merely a cheap battlecruiser
segments.summary: The class is valuable because it can support wartime forces while serving as Britain's visible peacetime presence abroad.
segments.body: The argument is not that Tiger Cubs should replace every battlecruiser mission. Their particular value lies in peacetime global presence, where they can reinforce cruiser forces and keep expensive battlecruisers concentrated for the North Sea while still demonstrating British interest and capability overseas.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts a global-presence mission with battlecruiser duties.

segments.title: Why Blucher would be outclassed
segments.summary: Against a 28-knot Tiger Cub, SMS Blucher's 25.4-knot speed and eight-gun broadside leave it at a severe tactical disadvantage.
segments.body: Although Blucher carries twelve 8.3-inch guns, her wing-turret layout limits a broadside to eight, directly comparable in number to the proposed British battery. The larger 9.2-inch shell and a 2.6-knot speed advantage let the British ships choose range and exploit more modern boilers, engines, and potentially oil firing.

evidence.note: The transcript compares Blucher's speed and effective broadside with the proposed ships.

segments.title: A test case for faster Queen Elizabeths
segments.summary: Successful pre-war machinery trials could make faster Queen Elizabeth and R-class designs more plausible in this alternate history.
segments.body: Because the Tiger Cubs would be laid down before the Queen Elizabeth class, their small-tube boilers could become a live Royal Navy experiment rather than a paper possibility. Dr. Clarke suggests that, with hull-length and other changes, the evidence might support substantially faster fast battleships and reshape later design choices.

evidence.note: The transcript connects successful boiler trials with higher prospective speeds for the Queen Elizabeth and R classes.

segments.title: A different Coronel with fast reinforcements
segments.summary: A pair of 28-knot cruisers with Cradock's squadron could harry von Spee, alter the range fight at Coronel, and change later British choices.
segments.body: The likely early posting is to Cradock's force after Goeben and Breslau escape, since the ships are fast enough to reinforce distant stations without sacrificing North Sea battlecruisers. They could outrun and outgun the German armoured cruisers, make it harder for von Spee to disengage, and allow a fast British group with Glasgow to dictate the pursuit rather than accept the historical battle on German terms.

evidence.note: The transcript proposes a Cradock deployment, compares speeds and guns, and explores the alternative Coronel engagement.

segments.title: Why the Courageous class may never be needed
segments.summary: If the Tiger Cubs prove their value, they could undermine both the Baltic rationale and the resources used for the Courageous class.
segments.body: The ships would be cheaper to build and crew than battlecruisers while still supplying powerful, fast support across the empire. Their existence weakens the case for special shallow-draft capital ships for the Baltic, and Dr. Clarke argues that Fisher would be less likely to cancel them or to pursue the Courageous, Glorious, and Furious as historical substitutes.

evidence.note: The transcript links a successful Coronel alternative to Fisher's procurement choices and the Baltic plan.
The discussion considers class size, wartime batches, and the consequences for available 15-inch turrets.

segments.title: Dogger Bank with a faster British force
segments.summary: At Dogger Bank, several 28-knot cruisers could give the British the speed, firepower, and command depth to pursue beyond Blucher.
segments.body: Once recalled from overseas hunting, the ships fit naturally with the battlecruiser force because they share Tiger's projected speed. Dr. Clarke argues that a three-knot advantage over a German force constrained by Blucher would let British ships set the range, leave a pair to finish Blucher, and keep the main pursuit moving instead of repeating the historical break in the chase.

evidence.note: The transcript assesses available numbers, the speed differential, command arrangements, and possible German losses at Dogger Bank.

segments.title: Could Cradock command the Tiger Cubs at Dogger Bank?
segments.summary: A surviving, victorious Cradock could return as the aggressive senior cruiser admiral whose independent signals keep the pursuit moving.
segments.body: The question asks which experienced cruiser admiral might lead the new squadron after the alternate victory at Coronel. Dr. Clarke identifies Christopher Cradock as a particularly plausible choice because success would earn promotion and recall with the ships. He distinguishes Cradock's aggressive leadership and cruiser-command experience from von Spee's stronger strategic gifts. At Dogger Bank, that temperament and independent authority could matter when deciding whether to stop at Blucher or continue after the German battlecruisers.
segments.question: Which cruiser admiral might command the Tiger Cub squadron after Coronel?
segments.answerShort: Christopher Cradock is a strong candidate if he wins at Coronel, returns with the ships, and is promoted to lead their squadron.

evidence.note: The transcript proposes Cradock as squadron commander and assesses his aggressive cruiser leadership.

segments.title: Gallipoli, raider hunting, and layered deterrence
segments.summary: The ships could reinforce the Mediterranean, hunt surface raiders, and restore an intermediate level of British power between light cruisers and battlecruisers.
segments.body: Pairs deployed with the Lord Nelsons could make it much harder for Goeben to exploit her speed, while others could serve with the Grand Fleet or roam against surface raiders. The underlying force-structure argument is that the Royal Navy had lost the armoured cruiser's overseas deterrent tier and was otherwise forced to choose between a light cruiser and the costly escalation of detaching a battlecruiser.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Gallipoli, Goeben, and world-wide raider-hunting roles.
The transcript explains the missing station-cruiser layer in Britain's deterrent hierarchy.

segments.title: Why Germany could not simply match the Tiger Cubs
segments.summary: German yards and budgets were already committed, so matching Britain's cruisers would require sacrificing a battleship or battlecruiser.
segments.body: The question asks whether Germany would answer the British programme with additional Blucher-like ships. Dr. Clarke argues that the High Seas Fleet's unbalanced structure reveals hard financial and yard limits rather than a failure to recognize useful cruiser roles. Germany lacked Britain's spare battleship-building capacity, and a new cruiser would displace a ship already valued more highly in its battle fleet. The asymmetry is therefore industrial as well as tactical: Britain can add a layer of force structure that Germany cannot copy without an explicit capital-ship tradeoff.
segments.question: Would Germany build one or two more Blucher-like cruisers in response?
segments.answerShort: Probably not: German money and yards were already committed, so each such cruiser would likely replace a battleship or battlecruiser.

evidence.note: The transcript answers that German budget and yard constraints would force a capital-ship tradeoff.

segments.title: Layered deterrence from patrol ships to carrier groups
segments.summary: A modern Royal Navy analogy explains why deterrence needs graduated levels of presence rather than a choice between light ships and a carrier group.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke compares the lost armoured-cruiser tier with a modern ladder running from offshore patrol vessels through Type 31 frigates, Type 26 and Type 45 escorts, and finally amphibious or carrier groups. Each step communicates a stronger degree of interest and brings more capability without immediately choosing the highest level of force. Removing the intermediate ship makes routine deterrence harder because every crisis demands either an inadequate light presence or an expensive major deployment. The Tiger Cub's strategic value is therefore institutional: it restores an option that lets naval power escalate visibly and proportionately.

evidence.note: The transcript uses OPVs, Type 31, Type 26, Type 45, and carrier groups to explain graduated deterrence.

segments.title: Fire control gains and machinery wear
segments.summary: Modern fire control would improve shore bombardment, but wartime high-speed use would leave these young cruisers needing major machinery work after 1918.
segments.body: A 9.2-inch ship designed in Tiger's era would likely bring better gunnery control than many older ships available for amphibious support, where precision matters to friendly troops as much as against enemy vessels. Dr. Clarke also expects sustained wartime service to wear out machinery well before the hulls, making a post-war re-engineering programme likely even for ships only a few years old.

evidence.note: The transcript links modern fire control to shore bombardment and estimates severe machinery wear in wartime service.

segments.title: Jutland with a weaker German scouting force
segments.summary: The video imagines Jutland after a much harsher Dogger Bank, leaving Germany with fewer battlecruisers against a faster and deeper British force.
segments.body: If Dogger Bank has removed several German battlecruisers and senior commanders, the First Scouting Group enters 1916 dangerously weak. Tiger Cubs would add speed, guns, and an additional senior command element to British formations, while faster Queen Elizabeths could make the German choice to risk a scouting action even less attractive.

evidence.note: The transcript explores altered German construction, British force composition, and the possible absence of a historical Jutland battle.

segments.title: Early carriers and raids on Wilhelmshaven
segments.summary: Without the historical battlecruiser losses and conversions, Britain might order purpose-built carriers earlier and use Tiger Cubs to protect offensive carrier raids.
segments.body: The video sees earlier carrier construction as a possible consequence of a different Jutland, with a larger purpose-built design developing from the Hermes concept. Tiger Cubs would be valuable carrier escorts: fast enough to avoid most heavy threats, strong enough to deter lesser forces, and capable of helping a raiding force threaten a harbour such as Wilhelmshaven before its defences adapted.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses early purpose-built carriers and a larger Hermes-derived design.
The transcript links cruiser escorts to carrier raids and the strategic effects of a Wilhelmshaven attack.

segments.title: Hawkins and a different post-war cruiser category
segments.summary: A successful Tiger Cub lineage would make the Hawkins class a next-generation 9.2-inch cruiser and force treaty makers to regulate a middle category of warship.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that Hawkins would not appear in its historical form if Britain already possessed a credible series of powerful station cruisers. The Washington system would then need to address ships between ordinary light cruisers and capital ships, likely with a tonnage and calibre ceiling rather than leaving every cruiser below capital-ship status unlimited.

evidence.note: The transcript connects the class's survival to Hawkins development and a different Washington Treaty structure.
The transcript discusses possible middle-category limits, nomenclature, and treaty allocations.

segments.title: Why 9.2-inch guns remain the preferred battery
segments.summary: The British would likely retain 9.2-inch guns because a 10-inch weapon adds weight and sacrifices rate of fire without enough compensating value.
segments.body: A treaty might round the category's calibre limit to 10 inches, but the Royal Navy's practical design choice remains 9.2 inches. The smaller gun can sustain a more useful rate of fire and makes larger batteries or better protection more achievable within an 18,000- to 20,000-ton cruiser than a heavier 10-inch system would allow.

evidence.note: The transcript compares 9.2-inch and 10-inch batteries within the proposed treaty category.

segments.title: An interwar race in carriers and station cruisers
segments.summary: A treaty that recognizes large cruisers and more carriers would redirect interwar competition away from the historical capital-ship race.
segments.body: The proposed system would leave Britain with existing ships that can be retained or replaced, while the United States, Japan, France, and Italy would have to invest in new designs. That changes the competitive focus from a narrowly constrained dreadnought race to the quality and number of carriers, high-end cruisers, and the industrial capacity to build them.

evidence.note: The transcript traces different carrier, cruiser, and capital-ship allowances through the interwar balance.

segments.title: The cruiser-and-destroyer carrier screen
segments.summary: Royal Navy carrier doctrine normally paired a carrier with a cruiser and destroyers; the Tiger Cubs make that screen more formidable.
segments.body: The video treats an unescorted carrier as a departure from the intended operating pattern rather than the normal arrangement. A carrier force built around a cruiser and several destroyers can handle routine movement and raider hunting, while a Tiger Cub gives the screen enough speed and firepower to operate with more confidence against surface threats.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the intended cruiser and destroyer escort arrangement for carriers.

segments.title: French and Italian allowances reshape the treaty balance
segments.summary: Giving France and Italy their own high-end cruiser tonnage could make the treaty more equitable while forcing both navies into a new design competition.
segments.body: The alternative category cannot be reserved for Britain, the United States, and Japan. Dr. Clarke estimates that France and Italy might each receive allowance for roughly nine ships under a proportional tonnage formula. That would redirect their construction choices and alter the relationship between cruiser, carrier, and capital-ship quotas across the treaty system. A broader distribution of useful modern tonnage might also make the settlement feel more equitable and therefore more durable than a framework focused almost entirely on battleships.

evidence.note: The transcript calculates possible French and Italian allowances and considers a more equitable treaty.

segments.title: How large would light cruisers become?
segments.summary: A chat question explores the lower category of cruisers once high-end station cruisers are regulated separately.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke suggests a light-cruiser ceiling of roughly 10,000 tons and 6.1-inch guns, with ordinary Royal Navy examples tending toward about 8,000 tons. Designers could still create powerful 10,000-ton ships with multiple triple turrets, while the separate 9.2-inch category preserves a more meaningful distinction in role and force structure.
segments.question: What would a light cruiser look like under this alternative treaty system?
segments.answerShort: Likely up to about 10,000 tons and 6.1-inch guns, with an 8,000-ton Royal Navy norm and room for much stronger all-gun designs at the ceiling.

evidence.note: The transcript directly answers the proposed size and armament of alternative light cruisers.

segments.title: Could carrier limits accelerate a dual-purpose 6-inch gun?
segments.summary: A question about heavily armed carriers leads to a possible earlier demand for a carrier-appropriate dual-purpose 6-inch weapon.
segments.body: Rather than put 10-inch guns on carriers, Dr. Clarke considers a treaty system that limits carrier guns to the armament allowed for unlimited lower-category ships. That could encourage development of a 6-inch weapon useful both for surface fire and anti-aircraft work, with consequences for later cruiser and carrier designs.
segments.question: Would alternative treaty rules put 10-inch guns on carriers?
segments.answerShort: Probably not: a 6-inch limit is more plausible, and it could encourage an earlier dual-purpose 6-inch gun for carriers and cruisers.

evidence.note: The transcript considers 6-inch carrier armament and an earlier dual-purpose gun.

segments.title: Why 9.2 inches is the cruiser sweet spot
segments.summary: A direct question on the ideal Tiger Cub battery prompts an explanation of why 9.2-inch guns offer the best balance of shell weight and firing rate.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that a 10-inch gun enters a slower firing-rate class comparable to 12-inch weapons, making it an awkward compromise. A 9.2-inch gun retains a rate more like the 7.5- and 8-inch class while throwing the heaviest shell available before the loading problem changes category, so it gives a cruiser a more useful balance of weight and volume of fire.
segments.question: What would be the ideal gun calibre for these cruisers?
segments.answerShort: Nine-point-two inches: it is the heaviest gun that still retains a markedly better rate of fire than the 10- to 12-inch class.

evidence.note: The transcript answers a question about the best calibre and compares firing-rate bands.

segments.title: How 9.2-inch shells compare with 8-inch shells
segments.summary: The larger 9.2-inch shell carries more explosive and normally reaches farther than an 8-inch shell in a comparable weapon.
segments.body: In answer to a question about bombardment value, Dr. Clarke emphasizes that the 9.2-inch round is simply larger and brings a heavier explosive payload. Combined with comparable contemporary technology, that makes it a more useful cruiser gun where range and destructive effect matter, rather than merely a marginal increase over 8 inches.
segments.question: How would 9.2-inch shells compare with 8-inch guns in a bombardment role?
segments.answerShort: They are larger, carry more explosive, and generally offer longer range in a comparable mounting.

evidence.note: The transcript directly compares 9.2-inch and 8-inch shell effect and range.

segments.title: Why 10-inch loading is a different problem
segments.summary: The dividing line is the machinery needed to handle a 10-inch shell, not a mysterious change in the gun's tactical role.
segments.body: The answer attributes the step change to shell and handling-system weight. Once a gun reaches the 10-inch class, its power loading and ammunition movement become sufficiently heavier that it no longer keeps the practical firing rhythm of the 8- and 9.2-inch range.
segments.question: Why is there such a large step in rate of fire between 9.2-inch and 10-inch guns?
segments.answerShort: The heavier 10-inch shell requires a substantially heavier handling and power-loading system, moving it into a slower firing-rate class.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the loading-machinery weight threshold.

segments.title: Torpedoes, submarines, and the German response
segments.summary: A larger-cruiser race might prompt changes in German torpedo and submarine priorities, but the direction depends on what Germany chooses to fund.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the answer as an open design-and-production question rather than a mechanical result. If Germany tries to match the ships, the cost could affect torpedo development; if its surface battle fleet becomes less relevant sooner, it may instead increase the attraction of submarines earlier in the war.
segments.question: Would larger ships drive bigger torpedoes and submarines, or earlier explosive improvements?
segments.answerShort: Possibly, but it depends on whether Germany tries to match the cruiser force or shifts resources earlier toward submarines as its battle fleet loses relevance.

evidence.note: The transcript weighs torpedo development against a possible earlier submarine emphasis.

segments.title: 9.2-inch firepower versus 6-inch cruisers
segments.summary: The 9.2-inch gun is presented as a historically grounded sweet spot whose effective fire can rival much larger 6-inch cruiser batteries.
segments.body: The argument rests on shell weight, rate of fire, and the design thresholds created by hull size and ammunition handling. Eight or nine 9.2-inch guns can deliver explosive effect comparable to a larger 6-inch battery, while still preserving a distinct role above the unlimited smaller cruiser category and below capital-ship armament.

evidence.note: The transcript explains gun-calibre sweet spots and compares 9.2-inch and 6-inch effective firepower.

segments.title: Why carrier air groups encounter a human ceiling
segments.summary: Larger treaty conversions would reveal that coordinating roughly one hundred aircraft creates an operational limit beyond simple deck area.
segments.body: The question asks how a more generous conversion allowance might change ships such as Kaga. Dr. Clarke argues that larger experimental carriers would help navies discover sooner that air-group size is constrained by people and coordination as well as hull dimensions. Once the complement approaches ninety to one hundred aircraft, launch, recovery, parking, maintenance, and command interactions become much harder to manage. That lesson could redirect design toward larger aircraft or more workable air groups rather than indefinitely increasing the number carried.
segments.question: How would a larger carrier-conversion allowance affect ships such as Kaga?
segments.answerShort: It could reveal earlier that air groups near one hundred aircraft face a human and operational management ceiling, encouraging different carrier and aircraft choices.

evidence.note: The transcript links larger carrier experiments with the human complexity of managing ninety to one hundred aircraft.

segments.title: Would Britain still need a York-class equivalent?
segments.summary: Britain could retain and replace its existing Tiger Cub lineage instead of building a cheaper York-class equivalent.
segments.body: The proposed treaty treatment gives the Royal Navy a durable baseline force of older ships that can remain in service until replacement is convenient. That reduces the pressure to build a cheaper intermediate cruiser merely to maintain numbers, because the earlier class itself continues to fill the role.
segments.question: Would Britain build a York-class equivalent with three triple 9.2-inch turrets?
segments.answerShort: Probably not: the Royal Navy could keep the older Tiger Cub lineage in service and replace it directly when needed, reducing the need for a cheaper equivalent.

evidence.note: The transcript directly answers whether a York-class equivalent would be necessary.

segments.title: A protected 9.2-inch cruiser design
segments.summary: A chat question prompts a concrete sketch of armour and underwater protection for the proposed class.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes a six-gun design within roughly 12,500 tons that carries a 7-inch belt, a 4.5-inch armoured deck, an internal protective deck, and a twin-hull anti-flooding arrangement. A larger 18,000-ton version could support at least nine guns while retaining substantially stronger protection and survivability features.
segments.question: What armour and torpedo protection could these alternative cruisers carry?
segments.answerShort: One 12,500-ton sketch carries a 7-inch belt, 4.5-inch deck, internal protective deck, and twin-hull anti-flooding; an 18,000-ton ship could add more guns and protection.

evidence.note: The transcript describes a concrete Ultimate Admiral design and its protection scheme.
